weather_line: Fog in the ridgelines · duck activity elevated · hold your hats

stories.headline: The Duck Bandit Revealed: An Identity, a Final Duck, and a Game That Was Never Quite What Anyone Thought
stories.byline: By our Investigative Affairs correspondent, with contributions from the Trail Desk
body: At approximately seven o'clock this morning, the Duck Bandit walked up Christine and Steve's front path, knocked twice, and introduced herself. Her name is Margaret Orvell, she is sixty-three years old, she has been driving modified Jeeps since before Steve was born, and she was, until recently, entirely retired from competitive trail-finding. 'Competitive' was her word. She used it without embarrassment.

Orvell explained the game to Christine over tea — there are rules, apparently, though they are unwritten and enforced only by reputation. The ducks are a greeting. The notes are a challenge. Finding the second overhang at Heron Shelf, which Orvell herself had spent two seasons trying to locate, had earned Christine what she called 'the last duck,' which she produced from her jacket pocket: a small brass-coloured duck with a red bandana, inscribed on the base with the coordinates of a ridgeline that does not appear on any standard chart.

Christine examined it for eleven seconds, asked two questions about access, and looked at Steve. Steve looked at the map. The map already had a fold in that direction. 'I know,' he said. Mrs. Orvell finished her tea and said she'd see them there, then, and left. The Investigation Board has formally closed the file, with honours.

stories.headline: 'Disputed (Atmospheric)': Steve Explains the Third Fold, and the Explanation Raises Further Questions of the Interesting Kind
body: On Tuesday evening Christine walked past the map a third time and stopped. Steve, who had been waiting with what he would describe as patience and the map would describe as anticipation, confirmed that yes, the ridgeline marked 'Disputed (Atmospheric)' is the destination. He had known about it since March. He did not mention it in March because March had felt, to him, too early.

'Disputed' refers to the fact that two survey teams in 1962 produced irreconcilable elevation readings for the same ridge, four feet apart, and neither team ever went back. 'Atmospheric' is a notation used when the discrepancy cannot be explained by instrument error. Steve said he has a theory about the gap. Christine asked him to rate his confidence on a scale of one to ten. He said seven. She said she needed the full survey notes. He produced them from beneath the map. He had printed them in March.

The Adventure Desk notes that Orvell's brass duck carries coordinates that correspond almost exactly to the disputed ridgeline. The Third Fold and the Bandit's final duck appear to be, in short, the same destination. This has not yet been said aloud in the household. Both parties are clearly aware of it.

stories.headline: Wednesday Morning Queue Resolves in Twenty-Six Minutes; Three Account-Holders Call It 'The Fastest Reckoning of My Life'
body: Wednesdays at Gringotts have historically featured a queue that regenerates at roughly the rate it is resolved, a phenomenon the goblins attribute to acoustics and the Senior Liaison attributes to clients who do not read their letters. This Wednesday, Christine arrived two minutes early, reorganised the morning's files by urgency rather than the order in which they were submitted, and had cleared the queue by half past nine.

Ragnok, passing through on his way to a governance session, paused at the door of the third consultation chamber, observed the situation, and nodded once — the goblin equivalent, multiple witnesses confirm, of a standing ovation. Christine was already on the next file. The Goblin Council has requested that this Wednesday's throughput figures be included in the quarterly benchmarking report, listed under Exceptional Events, and not compared to any other employee's data.

stories.headline: New Object in the House; Three Familiars File Conflicting Risk Assessments; Brass Duck Currently Under Surveillance from All Sides
body: The brass duck bearing Mrs. Orvell's coordinates was set on the kitchen table upon her departure and has not been moved since. It has, however, been assessed. Lucy circled it four times without touching it, which the Creature Desk considers a threat evaluation of the highest professional standard — she has not ruled out usefulness, and is reserving judgment until she determines what, if anything, it opens. Ellie approached it from the wrong end, concluded it was not food, and sat down next to it in case that turned out to be incorrect. Jersey has not approached it at all but has selected a napping position from which the duck is visible, which he is calling due diligence.
The duck has not moved, glowed, or emitted sound. It is, by all accounts, an entirely ordinary brass duck. The household is unconvinced.

familiars_corner: Lucy: The brass duck smells of trail, river clay, and a secret — Lucy has catalogued all three and intends to revisit the matter after the humans have gone to bed, at which point she will determine whether it is edible, openable, or simply worth sitting on.

Ellie: Ellie approached the duck a second time from the correct end and found it still not food, but has decided it is a friend on the grounds that it has not done anything alarming, which is her threshold for friendship and she stands by it.

Jersey: Jersey has identified that the warmest patch of morning light has shifted by six inches due to the open front door, adjusted his position accordingly, and returned to the important work of Wednesday.
founding_editor_note: A brass duck with coordinates on the base — now that is a well-built invitation. Whoever engraved that had a steady hand and a good level. I'd have done the same. — D.

in_brief.sport: Gringotts Weightlifting Invitational confirms all entries are, per Christine's pre-submitted inquiry, properly classified by verified total and not by what anyone 'feels like they could probably do.'
in_brief.forecast: Disputed (Atmospheric) conditions expected over northern ridgelines; elevations may vary by up to four feet depending on which survey team you believe; pack for both.

storyline_updates.title: The Duck Bandit
storyline_updates.summary: Resolved Wednesday morning: Margaret Orvell, 63, experienced trail-finder and retired competitive duck-placer, knocked on the front door and explained everything. The ducks were a greeting; the notes were a challenge; Christine earned the final duck — brass, red bandana, coordinates on the base — by finding the second overhang at Heron Shelf. The coordinates on the duck match the Third Fold's disputed ridgeline almost exactly. Orvell said she'd see them there. Arc closed with honours.

storyline_updates.title: The Third Fold
storyline_updates.summary: Steve has briefed Christine on the full story: a 1962 four-foot elevation dispute between two survey teams, both irreconcilable, both unreturned. He rates his own theory at seven out of ten. Christine has the survey notes — he printed them in March. Neither party has yet said aloud that Orvell's brass duck coordinates and the Third Fold appear to point to the same ridgeline, but both parties are aware of it. Day 3 should cover that acknowledgment and the first real planning — or an unexpected complication at the disputed ridgeline that the brass duck's coordinates hint at.
